era_context: The early 15th century saw the Ming Dynasty consolidating power under the Yongle Emperor, the Ottoman Empire expanding in the Balkans, the Great Schism in the Western Church creating ecclesiastical crisis, and the Hussite movement building momentum in Bohemia. The Zheng He treasure voyages were in their early phases. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire was consolidating power, and the Inca Empire was expanding.

events.title: Zheng He's Second Treasure Voyage departs from China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He, commanding a massive fleet of over 100 vessels and approximately 27,000 personnel, departed on his second major voyage of exploration in 1407 (some sources date this to 1409). The expedition sailed to Southeast Asia, India, the Arabian Peninsula, and East Africa, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Ming naval supremacy. The voyage lasted approximately three years and returned in 1410.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The departure date of the second voyage is disputed; some sources place it in 1407, others in 1409. Dating is uncertain by ±2 years.

events.title: Council of Constance preparations and ecclesiastical tensions
events.description: In 1407, preparations were underway for the Council of Constance (1414–1418), which would address the Great Schism and heresy in the Western Church. Ecclesiastical tensions were escalating as the Council of Pisa (1409) approached. The Church was divided among three papal claimants, and pressure was mounting for a general council to resolve the crisis.
key_figures: Sigismund, Holy Roman Emperor (future convener of Council of Constance)
events.certainty_note: Preparations for the Council of Constance were underway in 1407, but no specific events are documented for this year. The council formally opened in 1414.

events.title: Jan Hus's reformist preaching gains influence in Prague
events.description: Jan Hus, the Bohemian religious reformer, was gaining influence through his preaching at the Bethlehem Chapel in Prague in 1407. His teachings emphasized church reform, Czech national identity, and the authority of scripture, challenging papal authority and ecclesiastical corruption. Hus's influence was growing among the Bohemian nobility, clergy, and common people, setting the stage for his appointment as rector of Charles University in 1409.
key_figures: Jan Hus (Reformer, preacher at Bethlehem Chapel)

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussites

events.certainty_note: Hus's growing influence in 1407 is inferred from broader historical context; specific events for this year are not well-documented.
cross_references: 1409-003 (Jan Hus becomes rector of Charles University)

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates Balkan territories
events.description: The Ottoman Empire was consolidating its control over the Balkans in 1407, expanding its territorial holdings and establishing administrative structures. The empire was in a phase of consolidation and preparation for further expansion. Specific events for 1407 are not well-documented, but the empire was strengthening its position in the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in 1407 is not specifically documented; this entry reflects the broader context of Ottoman expansion during this period.

events.title: Aztec Empire consolidates power in the Valley of Mexico
events.description: The Aztec Empire was consolidating its power in the Valley of Mexico in 1407. The empire was expanding militarily and establishing tributary relationships with neighboring city-states. Specific events for 1407 are not well-documented in surviving sources, but the empire was strengthening its position in the region.
events.certainty_note: Specific events for 1407 in Aztec history are not well-documented in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader context of Aztec expansion during this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Zheng He's second voyage is disputed; some sources place the departure in 1407, others in 1409. The significance of 1407 as a departure date versus 1409 is not definitively established in modern scholarship. The entries on ecclesiastical preparations, Hussite influence, Ottoman consolidation, and Aztec expansion are all speculative; no major events are specifically documented for 1407 in these regions. These entries reflect broader trends rather than documented incidents for this specific year.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1407 is moderate but uneven. Chinese records are rich and contemporary, though the dating of Zheng He's voyages is disputed. European events (ecclesiastical tensions, Hussite influence) are less clearly documented for this specific year. Mesoamerican and South American history relies on post-conquest sources, introducing significant interpretive challenges and chronological uncertainty. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are largely absent from surviving written records. For 1407 specifically, the historical record is thin; most events are inferred from broader contextual trends rather than documented incidents.

graph_edges.note: Second Treasure Voyage in the series of Zheng He's expeditions
Second Treasure Voyage departure led to its return in 1410
Ecclesiastical tensions led to the Council of Pisa
Hus's growing influence led to his appointment as rector of Charles University
